title: Resistance Training
summary: Resistance training is the single highest-leverage exercise modality for longevity — it delivers cardiovascular benefits plus muscle preservation, which cardio alone cannot provide. 2 days/week minimum is sufficient to maintain the trajectory.

takeaways.headline: After 40, you lose ~1% muscle per year — only resistance training arrests it
takeaways.point: After age 40, muscle mass declines ~1% per year even in trained individuals; strength declines 1–3% per year. Sedentary people lose faster. Resistance training arrests this decline — it's the only modality that does.

takeaways.headline: Resistance training beats cardio: same heart benefits plus muscle
takeaways.point: If forced to choose one modality: resistance training over cardio. It provides most cardiovascular benefits (including VO2max improvement and mitochondrial health if programmed well) plus lean mass preservation that cardio cannot deliver.

takeaways.headline: Light weights to near-failure grow muscle as well as heavy
takeaways.point: Lighter weights to near-failure produce the same muscle growth as heavy weights. Practical implication: on low-energy days, go lighter but push to fatigue. Heavy training is only strictly necessary for pure strength goals.

takeaways.headline: 2 days/week full-body is the minimum effective dose
takeaways.point: Minimum effective dose: 2 days/week full-body. More is better but the marginal return flattens quickly. 150 min/week moderate cardio remains the baseline guideline alongside resistance work.

takeaways.headline: Never too late to start — gains are real at any age
takeaways.point: Never too late to start — meaningful gains are achievable at 80 or 100. But starting young builds a higher peak, which offsets the steeper age-related decline later.

takeaways.headline: Vigorous exercise is 4–10× more potent than moderate — guidelines are outdated
takeaways.point: Current exercise guidelines (150min moderate or 75min vigorous/week) were derived from a 2:1 calorie-burning ratio — not from disease outcome data. A new accelerometer-based study found: 1 min vigorous = 4 min moderate for all-cause mortality reduction; 1 min vigorous = 8 min moderate for cardiovascular mortality; 1 min vigorous = 10 min moderate for type 2 diabetes risk reduction. Light activity (walking around the house, 10,000 steps) provides negligible protection against most disease endpoints at any practical volume.

takeaways.headline: 3.5 minutes of vigorous activity daily cuts cancer risk 40%
takeaways.point: Exercise snacks — short bursts of vigorous movement spread across the day — count fully toward disease reduction. Women doing just 3.5 minutes/day of vigorous activity reduced cancer risk by 40%. Men and women doing 9 minutes/day total (spread as 1-minute bursts) achieved 40% lower cancer mortality and 50% lower cardiovascular mortality. Replace the 10,000 steps goal with a '10 minutes of elevated heart rate' target. Vigorous = approximately ≥70% max heart rate.

takeaways.headline: Sitting is an independent disease risk factor — even for people who exercise
takeaways.point: Sedentary time (time spent sitting) is an independent risk factor for cancer and cardiovascular disease, separate from whether you exercise. Exercising for an hour and then sitting for 8 hours does not cancel out the sitting. Breaking up sedentary time every hour with 1–2 minutes of movement meaningfully reduces this risk. Standing desks help but are not sufficient alone — movement is required.

takeaways.headline: 5 hours/week with some HIIT can reverse heart aging by 20 years
takeaways.point: Studies show that exercising approximately 5 hours per week with high-intensity interval training included can reverse cardiovascular aging by up to 20 years. This is the level required to maintain peak span — not just health span. For those who won't commit to this, 10 minutes/day of vigorous activity still provides substantial disease protection.

sources.title: Dr. Rhonda Patrick on Visceral Fat & Metabolic Health
